A Field Guide to Alpine Lakes

Alpine lakes form above the tree line, where glacial erosion carves basins that collect meltwater each spring. Their colour ranges from deep cobalt to milky turquoise depending on the concentration of suspended rock flour. This short guide shows the three lakes we surveyed last summer and what makes each of them distinctive for visitors and researchers alike.

The turquoise of Sorapis comes from finely ground dolomite held in suspension. The lake is fed entirely by snowmelt, so its level drops visibly over the course of the summer.

Braies is the largest natural lake in the Dolomites and the easiest of the three to reach, which makes early morning the only quiet time to photograph its famous boathouse.
